actions: Created pnpm-workspace.yaml for proper workspace configuration
Standardized all package.json scripts to use pnpm instead of npm
Added .npmrc with pnpm configuration (auto-install-peers, ignore-scripts:false)
Updated setup.sh script to auto-install pnpm and use it for all operations
Updated README with clear one-command setup and pnpm usage examples
Added packageManager field to enforce pnpm@10.27.0
Changed build script to sequential execution to handle dependency order

files.why: Configure pnpm behavior for native deps
Standardized all scripts to pnpm, added packageManager field
Auto-install pnpm, use it for all operations
Document pnpm usage and one-command setup

decisions.choice: Use pnpm exclusively instead of npm/yarn mix
decisions.reason: Eliminates workspace: protocol errors and provides better monorepo support

decisions.choice: Sequential build order instead of parallel
decisions.reason: Core must build first to generate types for server/tui/chat-web
Reduces friction - users only need Node.js installed

decisions.choice: Add preinstall hook to block npm
decisions.reason: Prevents accidental use of wrong package manager

TypeScript declaration files may not emit if tsconfig inheritance is misconfigured

Flush message.assistant before tool.call, skip at turn_end if flushed
TDD tests for linear event ordering

decisions.choice: Flush pre-tool content at first tool_execution_start
decisions.reason: Ensures message.assistant with tool_use comes BEFORE tool.call events for correct API ordering

decisions.choice: Use snake_case field names in tool_result content
decisions.reason: Anthropic API convertMessages expects tool_use_id and is_error in snake_case

decisions.choice: Flush pending tool results at end of getMessagesAt when last msg has tool_use
decisions.reason: Forked sessions need tool_result for tool_use blocks to satisfy API

snake_case vs camelCase field names must match what the provider conversion code expects

files.why: Add ToolUseBatchEvent type for batching tool intents
Emit tool_use_batch event before executing tools
Add registerToolIntents() for batch tool registration
Add unit tests for tool batching behavior

decisions.reason: Need to know ALL tool_use blocks BEFORE any execution starts, so flush at first tool_execution_start includes all tools
registerToolIntents() called on tool_use_batch adds all tools to tracker, then startToolCall() just updates status

lessons: When multiple tool_use blocks are in one turn, the model streams them ALL before execution starts. The agent has this info in toolCalls array but orchestrator only saw one-at-a-time via tool_execution_start events.
Solution: emit tool_use_batch event containing ALL tool intents before any execution, then orchestrator can register all and flush with complete message.assistant

front.title: Extract StreamAccumulator to consolidate LLM stream handler delta processing

body.input: Create shared StreamAccumulator in stream_common.rs to consolidate duplicated delta processing across Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google stream handlers
actions: Created stream_common.rs with StreamAccumulator struct and 32 unit tests
Migrated Anthropic stream_handler.rs to use StreamAccumulator (28 tests pass)
Migrated Google stream_handler.rs to use StreamAccumulator (17 tests pass)
Migrated OpenAI stream_handler.rs to use StreamAccumulator (29 tests pass)
Updated mod.rs with module registration and re-export
All 5724 tests pass with zero regressions

files.why: Shared StreamAccumulator for text/thinking/tool-call delta accumulation
Register stream_common module and add StreamAccumulator re-export
Replace raw accumulation fields with StreamAccumulator delegate
Replace raw accumulation fields with StreamAccumulator delegate
Replace raw accumulation fields with StreamAccumulator delegate

decisions.choice: StreamAccumulator supports both explicit-start (Anthropic) and implicit-start (OpenAI/Google) patterns
decisions.reason: Anthropic has explicit content_block_start events while OpenAI/Google detect first-delta; mark_text_started() vs process_text_delta() handles both

decisions.choice: Tool call handling stays partially provider-specific
decisions.reason: Google delivers complete function calls (not streamed deltas), OpenAI uses HashMap with parse_tool_call_arguments; these patterns diverge too much to fully unify

decisions.choice: State fields accessed via state.acc.* rather than wrapper accessors
decisions.reason: Direct field access is clearer than forwarding methods and matches Rust idioms

lessons: StreamAccumulator uses two modes: process_text_delta() auto-emits TextStart on first call; mark_text_started()+accumulate_text() for providers with explicit block-start events
Google tool calls are complete objects (not streamed), so StreamAccumulator tool_call flow only maps to Anthropic/OpenAI streaming patterns
When migrating state fields into a sub-struct, replace_all on field names is efficient but must also handle test setup code that directly assigns to old field names

decisions.choice: Extract tests to separate files rather than splitting production code into submodules
decisions.reason: Lower risk, same navigation benefit. Inline tests were the dominant size contributor (e.g., event_store.rs was 97 lines production + 2271 lines tests)
SwiftUI @State private vars cannot be accessed from extension files in separate files without breaking encapsulation

decisions.choice: Keep .isEmpty == false for optional chaining patterns
decisions.reason: skills?.isEmpty == false is correct — !skills.isEmpty would crash on nil. Also kept LoadedMemory.count > 0 since .count is a server-provided field, not a collection property

lessons: Rust inline test extraction: always check that head -n -1 doesn't strip the function closing brace along with the module closing brace — both use } at the same indentation after dedent
LoadedMemory.count is a server-provided integer field, NOT a Swift Collection.count — don't blindly replace with !isEmpty
SwiftUI @State private var is file-scoped — splitting views into extension files requires changing access to internal/fileprivate, which breaks encapsulation
Binary crate tests using use super::* need explicit imports when functions are moved to submodules
The #[path = "tests.rs"] #[cfg(test)] mod tests; pattern is the cleanest way to externalize Rust tests
